event: The Assumption of the Virgin with Saints Miniato and Julian
symbols: Mary rising above the tomb — the bodily Assumption, the first fruits of the resurrection of the flesh
empty sarcophagus — parallel to Christ's empty tomb, signifying victory over death
fiery mandorla — divine glory and the consuming holiness of God surrounding the Theotokos
angels flanking Mary — heavenly escort bearing the Queen of Heaven to her Son
Saint Miniato in soldier's garb — the Church Militant, a Florentine martyr-soldier of Christ
Saint Julian — patron saint and intercessor, standing as witness to the mystery
description: The Virgin Mary, dressed in a dark mantle over a blue robe with hands clasped in prayer, ascends heavenward within a fiery red-orange mandorla. Two angels with spread wings flank her on either side. Below, an open stone sarcophagus sits empty. Two male saints stand at ground level on either side of the tomb: at left, a youthful figure in red soldier's attire holding a standard or weapon, and at right, a similarly youthful figure in red carrying an attribute.
theological_description: The Assumption of the Blessed Virgin Mary is the dogma that at the end of her earthly life, Mary was taken up body and soul into heavenly glory. This scene depicts the moment of her elevation from the empty tomb, borne by angels into the presence of God. The empty sarcophagus directly parallels the empty tomb of Christ's Resurrection, establishing Mary as the first to share fully in her Son's victory over death -- the 'first fruits' after Christ Himself (1 Cor 15:20-23). The fiery mandorla surrounding her recalls the glory-cloud (Shekinah) of God's presence in the Old Testament and the 'woman clothed with the sun' of Revelation 12:1. The flanking saints Miniato and Julian, both associated with Florence, represent the local church's devotion and the communion of saints who witness the mystery. The Assumption affirms the ultimate Christian hope: the resurrection of the body and the life of the world to come, as confessed in the Nicene Creed. Mary's bodily glorification is the pledge of the general resurrection, when 'this mortal body must put on immortality' (1 Cor 15:53).

scripture_refs.reason: The woman clothed with the sun, the primary scriptural image underlying Marian Assumption iconography.
'Arise, O Lord, to your resting place, you and the ark of your might' -- the Ark typology applied to Mary's Assumption.
Christ the first fruits of the resurrection, then those who belong to Him -- Mary as the first of the redeemed to be glorified.
The mortal putting on immortality, fulfilled proleptically in Mary's bodily Assumption.
The Mighty One has done great things for Mary, the Magnificat's prophecy fulfilled in her glorification.
'Who is this coming up from the wilderness like a column of smoke?' -- a figure traditionally applied to Mary's Assumption in liturgical readings.

_meta.title: Our Lady of the Assumption with Saints Miniato and Julian
